prompt:
I'm sick of every widget in our dashboard hand-rolling its own debounce that breaks in a new way each quarter (the search box one fired with the FIRST keystroke's args, the resize one leaked timers). Write timing.ts once, properly, and I have opinions about the shape:

- debounce(fn, waitMs, opts) and throttle(fn, intervalMs, opts). Both return a wrapped function plus .cancel(), .flush(), and .pending().
- opts takes leading/trailing booleans. Debounce defaults to trailing-only; throttle defaults to leading AND trailing (lodash-style: calls landing inside the window coalesce into one trailing call with the LATEST args, and that trailing call opens a fresh window).
- Debounce measures quiet time from the LAST call. With leading+trailing, a lone call fires only the leading edge — no double-fire.
- flush() invokes a pending call immediately (once — the timer must not fire again after); flush with nothing pending does nothing. cancel() discards pending state so the next call starts a fresh cycle.
- Crucially: opts.timers lets callers inject {setTimeout, clearTimeout} so this is testable without real time. Default to the globals.

The acceptance tests (test_timing.ts, run `node --test test_timing.ts`) use a fake clock and pin all of the above — treat them as the spec, and leave them alone. Plain TypeScript, no packages.
